deepseek-r1-distill-llama-8b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this 67-year-old man.
Let me go through the case step by step.
He was initially admitted for hypoxemia.
Six years prior, he had melena and anemia, leading to an esophagogastroduodenoscopy which found duodenal ulcers.
The biopsy showed adenocarcinoma, so he was diagnosed with pancreatic adenocarcinoma with metastasis to the duodenum.
He had surgery, chemo, and radiation.
Then, two months later, a CT showed a pulmonary embolism in the right lower lobe.
He was treated with heparin but declined further treatment.
Over the next five years, he had surveillance CTs, and six months ago, a new mass in the sigmoid colon with lymphadenopathy was found.
Colonoscopy showed two masses, one at the ileocecal valve and another in the sigmoid.
Biopsy confirmed mantle-cell lymphoma.
He was started on chemo.
Five months ago, he developed right leg DVT and was treated with apixaban.
Then, he had R-DHAP chemo with filgrastim and acyclovir, but developed a rash, so switched to atovaquone.
Two days after chemo, he had melena, fatigue, and dyspnea.
His lab work showed anemia, so he got a blood transfusion.
He was started on omeprazole.
Then, he stopped atovaquone because of cost and started dapsone.
Three weeks later, another chemo cycle, and his DVT resolved.
Two weeks before presentation, fatigue and dyspnea recurred, but melena didn't.
Labs again showed anemia, he got another transfusion, and dyspnea didn't resolve.
On presentation, he had ongoing dyspnea, no cough, fever, etc.
He had loose stools since surgery.
His history includes hypertension and hereditary spherocytosis treated with splenectomy.
He drinks rarely, smoked 2 years, quit 20 years ago.
He's a lawyer, living alone, mother had breast cancer.
On exam, he's tachypneic but no increased respiratory effort.
No lung issues, legs normal.
Labs: WBC 21.56, which is high, but differential shows high neutrophils, which could be due to infection or chemo.
His LDH is high at 382, which is elevated.
The CT pulmonary angiography showed filling defects in the right upper and lower lobes, consistent with pulmonary emboli.
Tree-in-bud and ground-glass opacities in the upper lobes, which are probably inflammatory.
Small filling defects in subsegmental pulmonary arteries, consistent with pulmonary emboli.
The main pulmonary artery is 2.6 cm, which is enlarged.
He was admitted, started on IV heparin, and then presented with hypoxia.

The key here is the combination of pulmonary embolism, DVT, and the new lesions on CT. He's on chemo for mantle-cell lymphoma, which can cause immunosuppression, increasing the risk of infections and possibly other complications.
The high WBC and LDH could indicate infection or complications from chemo.
But the main issue now is the pulmonary findings.
The CT shows multiple pulmonary emboli, which is concerning for pulmonary embolism.
Given his history of DVT, this is a case of pulmonary embolism secondary to deep vein thrombosis.
The fact that he had a DVT and now pulmonary emboli is a sign of possible acute pulmonary embolism.
The treatment was started with heparin, which is standard for PE.
So, putting it all together, the most likely diagnosis is Acute Pulmonary Embolism.

Three weeks prior, he had another cycle of R-DHAP.
Two weeks before, he had fatigue and dyspnea again, with anemia.

Examining his labs, he had a low hematocrit and hemoglobin, high white cell count with lymphopenia, and elevated LDH.

The pulmonary embolism could be contributing to his hypoxemia, but he was on apixaban, which was stopped after the DVT resolved.

The LDH elevation could support an infectious cause.
Alternatively, the anemia could be due to hemolysis, but his history includes hereditary spherocytosis and splenectomy, which might not directly relate here.
Putting it all together, the most likely diagnosis is Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia.
His immunosuppression from chemotherapy makes him susceptible, and the imaging findings align with this diagnosis.
